﻿This congregation consists of three distinct branches, having the same end, nearly the same rules, the same vows of obedience, poverty and chastity, the same interests and government, viz: 1st. The "Priests of the Holy Cross," consecrated to the Sacred Heart of Jesus. Their constitutions are similar to those of St. Ignatius of Loyola; they devote themselves to missions and to education, in which they are assisted by the Brothers. Number of professed in this diocess [sic.], 8; novices 10.
2d. The Brothers consecrated to the Most Holy Heart of St. Joseph. Their institution is very similar, both as to its character and end, to that of the Christian Brothers, and they follow the same plan of instruction. They have been instituted chiefly to teach the poor and destitute, but they are also employed as assistant teachers and prefects at the college of the congregation, as directors of orphan asylums and manual labor schools, and to discharge the manual offices connected with a college and a farm. The most suitable age for admission is from fifteen to thirty-five, and the qualifications are, a blameless character, sound constitution, a true piety, pure morals, a firm and conciliating temper, and a great desire of sanctifying themselves in the line of obedience. When Brothers are sent on a mission, there must be at least two, each of whom must receive a salary of $150, per annum, unless the school is on their own account. Their novitiate is of one year, but they cannot make their perpetual vows before they are 21 years of age. Number of professed, 31; novices, 16; postualants, 9; number of boys in the manual labor school at Notre-Dame, 56.
3d. The "Sisters of the Holy Cross," consecrated to the Sorrowful and Immaculate Heart of Mary, form the third branch of the congregation, and unite the different vocations of the Ursulines and the Sisters of Charity, having charge of academies for the finished education of female youth, as well as of schools for the poor, manual labor schools, asylums and hospitals. Their novitiate is at St. Mary's, St. Joseph's county, Indiana, about two miles from Notre-Dame. This is also the site of their principal academy, and of the "Manual Labor School of the Immaculate Conception," founded for young girls between the ages of 12 and 18. Terms of admittance into either the male or female labor school, $50. They are then provided for until of age, and instructed in a useful trade and the ordinary branches of a common school education. Number of professed sisters, 33; novices, 38; postulants, 25. Number of girls in the manual labor school, 25.

instNote: ﻿This academy, lately removed from Bertrand, Michigan, is still under the direction of the Sisters of the Holy Cross, who have their novitiate at this place. This is a beautiful site, on a large farm recently purchased by the community, and combines the advantage of the pure and healthy air of the country, with the convenience of easy access from all our large cities, as it is only abour a mile from South Bend, through which the M.S.R.R. passes.
affiliated: ﻿Mission of the Sisters of the Holy Cross

instName: ﻿Community of St. Mary's of the Woods
instNote: ﻿The Sisters of Providence have their Mother-house at this place. Besides the academy and free school at St. Mary's, the Sisters have ten other establishments in the State.
